addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

lastPrompt: lets take a look at level 2. no one likes the walking up to the items because the board is hard to navigate. but i do want to send out subagents...its supposed to reflect when you have subagents do wo…

aiTitle: Improve level 2 navigation and subagent workflow

content.text:
I read through `droneBayView.js` end to end. Before throwing ideas out, here's the thing that jumps at me:

**You already solved this exact problem in Level 1.** The L1 rebuild ditched "walk around to items" for "stand in one place, things come to you, act from a fixed spot." L2 still has you trekking first-person across six far-flung coordinates `(-34,-28)`, `(40,-18)`, `(28,38)`… on bumpy terrain, under a clock. That's the navigation pain. The fix is the same move that already worked once.

And here's the lucky part: the subagent fiction *wants* you to stay put. Real delegation means **you don't go to the work — the work goes away from you.** So pinning the player down isn't a compromise, it's the metaphor.

## The core idea: Mission Control

You never leave the ship console. The console *is* the level — it's the "one dialogue-box channel" from your Bug Bash simplicity bar. The island becomes a thing you *watch*, not a maze you cross.

- **Dispatch from one spot.** A list of 5 dark systems at the console. Pick one, press E → a drone launches and flies off across the island. No walking. You can fire off all five in seconds.
- **The work happens *over there*, out of reach.** You literally watch drones working at distant beams you can't get to. That's the "behind the scenes" feeling made physical — you see *that* something's happening, not *what*.
- **The reveal comes home to you.** Drone returns with a sealed checkpoint. `entire checkpoint explain` runs right at the console. The recovery is the payoff, in your hands, where you already are.

## The piece I think you're actually reaching for: the *blind* reveal

You said the heart of it is "you don't know what they did until Entire recovers it." But the current code **shows the finished upgrade the moment it seals** (`upgradeModel.visible = true` on `sealSite`) — you can squint across the island and basically see the plasma ring before you ever run explain. That undercuts the whole emotional beat.

What if the returned work is genuinely **shrouded** — iced over, a featureless sealed pod — and `checkpoint explain` is the *only* thing that unwraps it? The drone comes back carrying a mystery box. Explain is the reveal. ADD TO SHIP is you accepting it. That turns explain from "extra step" into the dramatic high point — which is the lesson you're trying to teach.

Mission Control kills the walking; the blind reveal makes explain *matter*. They're independent, but together they're the level you're describing.

A lighter-touch alternative, if you don't want to rebuild the layout: keep the island but **dispatch from the overhead map** (M already exists) — click systems on the bird's-eye view to send drones, and only walk in to do reviews. Less radical, but it still leaves some trekking.

A couple of things I want to pin down before sketching either one:

questions.question: How far do you want to go on killing the navigation?

options.label: Full Mission Control (Recommended)
options.description: Player is anchored at the console the whole level. Dispatch + review + report all happen from one spot. Drones do all the moving. Closest to the L1 fix.

options.label: Dispatch from the map
options.description: Keep the island, but send drones by clicking the overhead map. Still walk in to review sealed sites. Smaller change.

options.label: Keep walking, just fewer/closer sites
options.description: Minimal: pull the 5 systems much closer to the console so the trek is short. Least work, least payoff.

questions.question: Should the subagent's work be hidden until you run `checkpoint explain`?

options.label: Yes — blind reveal
options.description: Returned work is shrouded/iced; explain is the only thing that unwraps what the drone actually built. Makes explain the emotional high point.

options.label: No — keep it visible
options.description: Upgrade shows as soon as it seals, like now. Explain stays informational rather than a reveal.

You can now continue with these answers in mind.

answers.How far do you want to go on killing the: i feel like there's different ways to think about this..like for example..in level 1 we are doing shooting
answers.Should the subagent's work be hidden unt: this shouldnt be something we're wondering about

content.text:
Got it — you're not asking "what layout," you're asking "what's the *verb*." L1's verb is **shoot**. L2 needs its own verb that *is* delegation — and yeah, the work being hidden until `explain` recovers it isn't a question, that's just the point of the level. So let me think in mechanics, not maps.

Here are genuinely different ways to frame the L2 verb:

**1. Launch & forget — shooting, inverted.**
Same kinetic feel as L1, flipped in meaning. In L1 you shoot to *bank what's yours*. Here you aim at a dark system and **fire a drone out** — you launch work *away*. You can spray all five out fast (that's the parallelism). Then you can't watch them. They come back as sealed checkpoints, and the *second* verb is cracking one open = `explain`. Shares L1's muscle memory, opposite meaning. Clean contrast to teach against.

**2. Catch the returns — make the recovery the game.**
This one leans hardest into your core ("you don't know what they did until Entire recovers it"). Sending is trivial — one button, drones swarm out. The *game* is what flies **back at you**, exactly like L1's falling records: sealed checkpoints come home and you have to `explain` each one to see what the subagent improvised, then decide keep/toss. The verb is *recovering under pressure*. The thing you said matters most literally becomes the gameplay.

**3. Dispatch board — juggling, not aiming.**
No aim at all. A control panel: systems on one side, a pool of drones. The fun is parallel juggling — fire jobs off, they return needing review, and you're explaining-and-accepting while still dispatching. This is the **Diner Dash** loop from L1's original design, reborn as air-traffic control. Skill = timing and overlap, which is *exactly* the real feeling of running multiple agents.

My lean: **#2**, with a dash of #1's launch. Because the whole reason this level exists is the recover-what-you-didn't-watch beat — so make *that* the thing the player's hands are doing, not a menu step after a walk. Sending should be a quick flourish; the drama is the return.

One thing that'd help me push the right one further — when you picture the *fun moment* in L2, is it the **chaos of sending a swarm out** (#1/#3), or the **payoff of cracking open work you didn't see** (#2)?

We're brainstorming the level's core verb; I pitched three options and leaned toward making the "recover work you didn't see" the gameplay. Next: hear which fun moment you want to build around. (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
Let me see what the player is actually staring at during that moment. Right now, when you're typing `git add`, the terminal stacks **four** text channels:

1. `#term-hint` — `# ADD` (12.5px, dim)
2. `#term-line` — `crashlog:~$ git add▮` (16px) ← the thing you type
3. `#term-cta` — `TYPE  git add` (the command shown *again*)
4. `#term-msg` — feedback (11.5px)

So the screen literally shows "git add" **twice** plus "ADD" a third time — and the biggest font in there is only 16px, behind an 11-character `crashlog:~$` prompt that eats the left third of the line before your command even starts. That's your "too small + too crowded": the hero (the command you type) is small, and it's drowned by redundant chrome saying the same thing three ways.

The fix is really two moves: **make the typed command the biggest thing in the box**, and **kill the duplication** around it. Here are three ways to land that:

questions.question: Which terminal redesign feels right for Level 1?

options.label: Ghost-text you type over
options.description: One big line. The target command shows as faded ghost text inside the input; it lights up bright as you type it correctly. No separate hint, no duplicate CTA. Cleanest — matches your 'one channel' bar.
options.preview:
┌────────────────────────────┐
  │                            │
  │   $ git add▮               │   ← 24px
  │     ░░░░░░░  (ghost target) │
  │                            │
  └────────────────────────────┘

 type over the faded command;
 it brightens as you match it.
 one line. nothing repeated.

options.label: Big line + shrink the chrome
options.description: Keep the structure but make the command ~24px, cut the prompt to '$', drop the '# ADD' hint, keep one quiet helper below. Smallest change to the code.
options.preview:
┌────────────────────────────┐
  │   $ git add▮               │   ← 24px, hero
  │                            │
  │   type to stage the record │   ← one helper
  └────────────────────────────┘

 prompt shrinks crashlog:~$ → $
 hint line removed
 command shown once, big

options.label: Card-style step
options.description: Reframe each step as a labeled card: a big STAGE / FREEZE / LINK heading, the command huge below it, feedback at the bottom. More structured, reads like a guided step.
options.preview:
┌────────────────────────────┐
  │  STEP 1 · STAGE            │
  │                            │
  │   git add▮                 │   ← 26px
  │                            │
  │  ·············             │
  └────────────────────────────┘

 big step label up top
 command is the body
 no shell prompt clutter

input.prompt:
You are helping redesign the Level 1 END/WIN screen of a Three.js browser game in this repo (/Users/goose-guest/Documents/work/planetfall-seed-signalkit).

Level 1 is a falling-records shooter implemented in src/islandView.js. When the level ends, a summary screen shows something like "Time… you recovered N records" etc. The user's complaint: the text on that end screen is TOO SMALL, the CONTAINER is too small, and maybe we shouldn't use that container/style at all — maybe it should be something else entirely.

Your job is INVESTIGATION + DESIGN PROPOSAL ONLY. Do NOT edit any files.

Do this:
1. Find the Level 1 end/win/results screen: its HTML markup (in index.html), the CSS that styles it (in src/style.css), and the JS that populates it (in src/islandView.js). Identify the exact element IDs/classes and the exact copy/text shown.
2. Report the current font sizes, container width/padding, and layout of that end screen — quote the real CSS values and the real text strings.
3. Note whether this container/class is SHARED with other screens (e.g. the fail screen, Level 2's win/fail in droneBayView.js, the briefing). This matters because changing shared styles affects multiple screens — flag any sharing explicitly.
4. Propose 2-3 concrete redesign directions to make it bigger / more readable / less cramped, OR to replace the container with something better. For each, give specifics (font sizes, layout, what changes). Keep it aligned with the game's existing visual language (CRT/terminal sci-fi, cyan accents — look at the existing styles).

Return a concise written report with file:line references, the quoted current values, the sharing analysis, and your proposals. Do not write code.

aiTitle: Improve level 2 navigation and subagent workflow

content.text: I have a complete picture. Here is my report. --- # Level 1 END/WIN Screen — Investigation & Redesign Proposal ## 1. There is no single "results container" for the Level 1 win This is the key finding. Unlike Levels 2 and 3, **Level 1's win has no dedicated win card.** When the player clears the minimum, `passRun()` (`src/islandView.js:715-720`) does NOT open a results panel. Instead it stitches together three existing HUD elements: 1. **`#tutorial`** — a thin top banner with the line: `"Time. ${banked} records recovered. Run \`entire checkpoint list\` to review your haul."` (`islandView.js:719`) 2. **`#terminal`** (bottom) — after the player types `entire checkpoint list`, `showCheckpointList()` (`islandView.js:686-702`) fills `#term-list` with one `.term-list-row` per record (`Entire-Checkpoint: IDrecovered: TITLE`) and flashes `#term-msg`: `"${banked} checkpoints linked · ship memory restored"`. 3. **`#level-mode-prompt`** in handoff mode — `showModePrompt("next")` shows the title-screen-style card with head `"LEVEL 1 COMPLETE"`, action button `"START LEVEL 2"`, note `"Ship memory restored — the drone bay just woke up."` (`MODE_PROMPTS.next`, `islandView.js:78-83`). So "the end screen text is too small" almost certainly refers to the **terminal haul list at the bottom** and/or the **tutorial line at the top** — both are genuinely tiny. The big readable element is only the mode-prompt button. The HTML markup lives at `index.html:131` (`#tutorial`), `index.html:138-147` (`#terminal` block), and `index.html:173-179` (`#level-mode-prompt`). The separate **fail** screen IS a real card: `#level-fail` (`index.html:207-211`), populated by `failRun()` (`islandView.js:721-728`) with title `"NOT ENOUGH MEMORY"` and sub `"You recovered ${banked}. The ship needs at least ${MIN_TO_PASS} to power up."`. ## 2. Current sizes / layout (real values) Color tokens (`style.css:2-4`): `--accent: #6fe3ff` (cyan), `--accent-warm: #ffb86b`, `--ink: #e8f4ff`. **`#tutorial`** (`style.css:1286-1304`): top-anchored at `top: 132px`, `max-width: min(560px, 100vw-48px)`, `padding: 10px 18px`, **`font-size: 12.5px`**, `line-height: 1.5`, faint `rgba(8,14,26,0.62)` bg. (Mobile override: `11.5px`, `style.css:1850-1854`.) **`#terminal`** (`style.css:1338-1352`): bottom-anchored `bottom: 70px`, `width: min(560px, 100vw-40px)`, `padding: 14px 16px`, cyan border. - `#term-line` prompt: `16px` (`style.css:1362-1367`) - `#term-hint`: `12.5px` (`style.css:1354`) - `#term-list`: `max-height: 168px`, scrolls; rows `.term-list-row` are **`font-size: 12.5px`** (`style.css:1465-1469`); `.tl-id min-width: 300px` - `#term-msg` (the "ship memory restored" line): **`font-size: 11.5px`**, `opacity: 0.92` (`style.css:1490-1499`) — this is the smallest, faintest text on the win screen. **`#level-mode-prompt`** (`style.css:803-865`): full-screen overlay. In `.is-handoff` mode the menu is top-anchored (`margin-top: clamp(48px,12vh,150px)`), gradient dims downward so the haul list stays visible. - Head "LEVEL 1 COMPLETE": `clamp(12px, 1.3vw, 15px)`, gold `#f6c763` (`style.css:841-848`) - Button "START LEVEL 2": `.level-mode-menu .ts-item` → `clamp(19px, 3vw, 30px)` (`style.css:852-856`) — the one large element - Note: `clamp(12px, 1.4vw, 15px)` (`style.css:858-864`) So the actual "you recovered N records" content is all rendered at **11.5–12.5px**, in a half-transparent bottom terminal box capped at 560px wide — that is the cramped/small feeling. ## 3. Sharing analysis (important — what's safe to touch) - **`#tutorial`** — SHARED. It is the single "dialogue-box channel" used everywhere in both island levels for teaching lines (`showTutorial()` is called all over `islandView.js`, e.g. lines 719, 973). Changing its font/size affects every tutorial beat, not just the win line. - **`#terminal` / `#term-list` / `.term-list-row` / `.tl-*` / `#term-msg`** — HEAVILY SHARED. Same terminal is used for the `git add` / `git commit` / link-checkpoint banking steps during normal play, AND it is reused by Level 2's Drone Bay / Archive views (`.tl-id-short` at `style.css:1476`, `.tl-exp-key` at `style.css:1478` are L2-specific row variants). Bumping `.term-list-row` font size changes the in-play terminal and L2 too. - **`#level-mode-prompt` / `.level-mode-*` / `.ts-item`** — SHARED within L1 (used for tutorial start, level start, and the handoff) and `.ts-item` is the title-screen menu button style used across the whole game (`style.css:120`). The `.is-handoff` variant (`style.css:818-830`) is win-specific, though. - **`#level-fail`** — SHARED selector group with `#l2-fail, #db-fail, #lc-fail` (`style.css:1115-1118`); `.lf-*` classes are L1-only in markup but the box rule is the shared red fail-card style. - **`#l2-win, #db-win, #lc-win` + `.lw-title`/`.lw-sub`** (`style.css:1239-1268`) — the green win-card pattern that Levels 2 and 3 DO have but Level 1 does NOT. This is the natural template to copy. Bottom line: almost everything currently composing the L1 win is shared, so the cleanest fix is to **add a dedicated L1 win card** rather than enlarge the shared terminal/tutorial text. ## 4. Redesign proposals ### Proposal A (recommended): Give Level 1 a real win card, matching L2/L3 Level 1 is the only level without its own win panel — it improvises one from shared parts, which is exactly why it reads small. Add an `#island-win` card modeled on the existing `#l2-win/#db-win/#lc-win` green card (`style.css:1239-1268`): - Box: `width: min(520px, 88vw)`, `padding: 30px 32px`, green border/glow (reuse the `.lw-*` look), centered. - Title `.lw-title`-style at **24-28px** (vs current 11.5px): `"SHIP MEMORY RESTORED"`. - Sub at **15-16px** monospace cyan: `"You recovered N records."` plus a small stat row. - Move the recovered-records list INTO this card as a styled `.win-haul` block at **14px** rows (its own class, not shared `.term-list-row`), with the cyan `Entire-Checkpoint:` IDs. - Footer button reuses `.ts-item` (already large) → `START LEVEL 2`. This keeps the win self-contained and readable, costs nothing to the in-play terminal, and makes L1 consistent with L2/L3. It does change the current "keep terminal list visible behind a handoff card" choreography — worth confirming with the user since that layered look was a deliberate design (memory note: keep the haul list visible behind the card). ### Proposal B (lowest risk): Win-only size overrides via the `.is-handoff` scope Keep the existing three-part composition but scope larger type to the win state only, so nothing else changes. Since the handoff adds `.level-mode-prompt.is-handoff`, but that's a separate element from the terminal, instead add a body/HUD state class (e.g. `#island-hud.is-review`) toggled in `passRun()`/`showCheckpointList()` and write: - `#island-hud.is-review .term-list-row { font-size: 15px; gap: 16px; }` - `#island-hud.is-review #term-msg { font-size: 14px; }` - `#island-hud.is-review #terminal { width: min(680px, 100vw-40px); padding: 20px 24px; }` - bump the `#tutorial` win line by scoping similarly. Pro: minimal, preserves the layered look. Con: still a terminal box, still bottom-anchored and semi-transparent; it's "less small" but not "something better." ### Proposal C (middle ground): Promote the haul list into a centered "manifest" panel Replace the bottom-terminal haul display on win with a centered, opaque CRT "MISSION RECORDS" manifest (its own element), while keeping the `#level-mode-prompt` button below or inside it: - Centered panel `width: min(560px, 90vw)`, solid `rgba(4,10,18,0.95)`, cyan border + scanline texture to match the CRT frame. - Header **22px** cyan `"MISSION RECORDS RECOVERED — N"`. - Record rows at **15px**, generous `gap: 8px`, each with a small gold record glyph. - `START LEVEL 2` button (`.ts-item`) docked at the bottom of the panel. This directly answers "maybe it should be something else entirely" — it reframes the haul as a proud end-of-mission manifest rather than terminal output, fits the sci-fi/CRT language, and uses bigger type throughout. More work than B, less templated than A. **My recommendation:** Proposal A for consistency with the rest of the game, or Proposal C if the user wants the win to feel distinct and more celebratory. Either way, define new classes rather than enlarging the shared `#tutorial`, `#terminal`, and `.term-list-row` styles, since those are used during play and in Level 2.

So "the end screen text is too small" almost certainly refers to the **terminal haul list at the bottom** and/or the **tutorial line at the top** — both are genuinely tiny. The separate **fail** screen IS a real card: `#level-fail` (`index.html:207-211`), populated by `failRun()` (`islandView.js:721-728`) with title `"NOT ENOUGH MEMORY"` and sub `"You recovered ${banked}. content.text:
Terminal change is done. Here's what Level 1's terminal now does:

- **Command line is the hero** — bumped 16px → **24px** (19px on mobile).
- **Prompt shrunk** `crashlog:~$` → `$`, so your command starts almost at the left edge instead of behind 11 characters of chrome.
- **Killed the duplication** — the cryptic `# ADD` hint is gone, and the duplicate `TYPE git add` chip is replaced by one quiet plain-language helper (`type to stage this record` / `…freeze it as a snapshot` / `…review your haul`). So `git add` now appears **once**, big, where you type it.

The bigger font carries into L2's terminal too (beneficial), and L2's review hints still work since they set real text.

---

Now the **end screen**, with the subagent's findings. The big discovery: **Level 1 has no real win screen.** L2 and L3 each have a proper win card; L1 fakes one by stitching together three shared pieces, all tiny:

- the **tutorial banner** at top (12.5px): *"Time. N records recovered…"*
- the **terminal haul list** at bottom (12.5px rows) + a *"ship memory restored"* line at **11.5px** — the smallest, faintest text on the screen
- the handoff button (the one big element)

That's exactly your complaint — small text in a cramped half-transparent terminal box. And the fix isn't "enlarge the terminal," because that terminal is shared with gameplay and L2; blowing it up there would wreck the typing UI. The right move is to give L1 its **own** win screen. Three ways to do it:

questions.question: How should Level 1's end screen work?

options.label: Mission-records manifest (Recommended)
options.description: Replace the bottom-terminal haul with a centered, solid CRT 'MISSION RECORDS' panel: big title + stat line (~24px), record rows at ~15px, START LEVEL 2 docked inside. Reframes the haul as a proud end-of-mission readout. Directly answers 'use something else'.
options.preview:
┌──── MISSION RECORDS ────┐
  │                          │
  │  4 RECOVERED · 1:12        │  ← ~24px
  │                          │
  │  ◈ a1c9  the key          │  ← 15px
  │  ◈ 3d7b  the wrench       │
  │  ◈ f25c  the telescope    │
  │  ◈ 62e0  the canister     │
  │                          │
  │     [ START LEVEL 2 ]     │
  └────────────────────────┘

options.label: Match L2/L3 win card
options.description: Give L1 a dedicated green win card cloned from L2/L3's existing style: big title 'SHIP MEMORY RESTORED', sub stat line, haul list inside, START LEVEL 2 button. Most consistent with the other levels, least net-new design.
options.preview:
┌────────────────────┐
  │ SHIP MEMORY RESTORED  │  ← ~26px green
  │                      │
  │ You recovered 4.     │
  │ · the key  · wrench   │
  │ · telescope · canister│
  │                      │
  │   [ START LEVEL 2 ]   │
  └────────────────────┘

options.label: Just enlarge in place
options.description: Keep the stitched-together approach but scope bigger fonts to the win state only (bigger haul rows, wider box, bigger banner). Lowest effort, but it's still a bottom terminal box — less 'something else', more 'less small'.
options.preview:
stays the bottom terminal box,
just:
  • haul rows 12.5 → 15px
  • box 560 → 680px wide
  • 'restored' line 11.5 → 14px
  • top banner a bit bigger

message.content: there's text overflow for link this record to a checkpoint yn so maybe we should do something for that..make the container bigger or something else idk

content.text: The overflow is because that confirm question is being forced into the 24px monospace command line with `white-space: pre` (never wraps). But that line is really meant for *commands you type* — the link step isn't typed, it's a Y/N prompt. So rather than just widening the box (which still won't wrap on narrow windows), let me render the question as its own readable, wrapping line without the `$` prompt/cursor, and let the Y/N keys be the action.

For the link step, the terminal now drops the `$` prompt and blinking cursor (it's a question, not something you type) and the question **wraps** instead of running off the edge — so "Link this record to a checkpoint?" sits on its own readable line at 24px with the **Y** / **N** keys below it.
